coredata.dc:title: Combined effect of cycle aging and temperature on the variation of the open-circuit voltage of lithium cobalt oxide batteries

coredata.dc:description: A key focus in studying lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) is the estimation of their actual capacity. To this end, many algorithms rely on the relationship between the open-circuit voltage (OCV) and state of charge (SOC) or absolute state of discharge, q. This relationship can be influenced by factors such as temperature (in a reversible way) and battery degradation (in an irreversible way). Although several studies investigated variations in the OCV-SOC or OCV-q relationship due to temperature or cycle aging using lookup tables or analytical expressions with adjustment factors, a comprehensive analytical model that simultaneously incorporates both factors and defines its parameters remains absent. To address this gap, the present work extends an existing analytical OCV-q model to capture variations in the OCV-q relationship as a function of both battery temperature and cycling level. To this aim, a comprehensive experimental campaign was conducted on a LiB, characterizing its OCV curve across various temperatures and cycling levels. Finally, simulations validated the accuracy of the proposed OCV-q model, yielding a mean relative OCV error below 0.8 % across all tests. Furthermore, the model demonstrated the ability to estimate the actual battery capacity with an estimation error of less than 2.5 % in all cases.

head.ce:title: Combined effect of cycle aging and temperature on the variation of the open-circuit voltage of lithium cobalt oxide batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A key focus in studying lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) is the estimation of their actual capacity. To this end, many algorithms rely on the relationship between the open-circuit voltage (OCV) and state of charge (SOC) or absolute state of discharge, q. This relationship can be influenced by factors such as temperature (in a reversible way) and battery degradation (in an irreversible way). Although several studies investigated variations in the OCV-SOC or OCV-q relationship due to temperature or cycle aging using lookup tables or analytical expressions with adjustment factors, a comprehensive analytical model that simultaneously incorporates both factors and defines its parameters remains absent. To address this gap, the present work extends an existing analytical OCV-q model to capture variations in the OCV-q relationship as a function of both battery temperature and cycling level. To this aim, a comprehensive experimental campaign was conducted on a LiB, characterizing its OCV curve across various temperatures and cycling levels. Finally, simulations validated the accuracy of the proposed OCV-q model, yielding a mean relative OCV error below 0.8 % across all tests. Furthermore, the model demonstrated the ability to estimate the actual battery capacity with an estimation error of less than 2.5 % in all cases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) are fundamental for advancing the goal of climate neutrality within the framework of the European Green Deal [1]. They are currently the preferred energy source for electric mobility and play a key role when combined with non-programmable renewable energy sources, delivering a variety of services to the grid [2–4]. Compared to other storage technologies, LiBs exhibit excellent performance in terms of energy and power density, efficiency, and lifetime [5]. However, like all electrochemical storage systems, they are subject to degradation, which reduces their capacity to deliver energy (capacity fade) or power (due to increased internal resistance). This degradation occurs under different conditions, which can be mainly divided into storage conditions (calendar aging) and cycling conditions (cycle aging) [6].

To limit degradation, it is essential to develop battery management systems (BMSs) that align with the actual status of the battery and can predict its behavior under specific conditions. Monitoring the battery status requires the BMS to precisely estimate two key parameters: the state of charge (SOC) and the state of health (SOH). The former ensures the battery operates within safe limits [7], while the SOH measures the battery degradation, typically expressed in terms of capacity fade or resistance increase [8].

Several methods exist for estimating the SOC. Among these methods, the Coulomb counting and estimation based on the open-circuit voltage (OCV) curve are the most widely adopted [9]. The OCV curve also plays a crucial role in estimating the battery capacity and, consequently, the SOH. Specifically, the OCV can be expressed as a function of SOC or as a function of the absolute state of discharge, q. In the latter case, the value of q at the minimum cutoff voltage represents the battery capacity [10].

Thus, understanding the OCV curve is essential for accurately predicting battery status, and various modeling approaches can be found in the literature. One approach is to store the OCV-SOC or OCV-q pairs in a lookup table, ensuring a computationally light model [11]. However, this method requires a lot of memory, depending on the number of pairs needed to achieve the desired precision, which are not always available. Another possibility is to model the curve using mathematical expressions. Among these, polynomials are the most commonly used, ranging from the 2nd to the 12th degree [12–14]. The accuracy of these functions increases with their degree, but the number of parameters to determine also increases. Additionally, other modeling approaches found in the literature use logarithmic [15] or exponential [16] functions, or a combination of different expressions [17,18].

The OCV curve changes as the battery condition evolves, particularly depending on factors such as temperature and aging. Therefore, understanding how the OCV curve varies with these factors is crucial. It is worth noting that, while temperature contributes to irreversible battery aging, it also has a reversible impact on battery performance by altering both internal resistance, capacity, and the OCV curve [19].

The impact of aging on the OCV curve has been widely investigated in the literature. In Ref. [20], the authors aged different lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries of the same type using various charging/discharging cutoff voltages and charging/discharging current rates, while keeping the temperature constant for all tests. They stated that with an appropriate adjustment of the SOC based on the actual capacity, the OCV curve remains unchanged with cycle aging. In Ref. [21], the authors aged different LiBs, nominally identical, using electric vehicle-like profiles. However, they did not specify the exact type of profiles or the temperature conditions under which the tests were performed. In any case, to adapt the initial OCV-SOC curve for the fresh battery to account for aging effects, an adjustment term dependent on the SOC and SOH was added. In Ref. [22], the authors tested an LFP battery undergoing cycle aging using a mixed driving cycle profile and exposed the battery to varying temperatures between 0 °C and 40 °C. Specifically, the OCV-SOC curve was updated by investigating an SOH-SOC function. In Ref. [17], the authors tested LFP batteries aged using the federal urban driving schedule profile. They proposed an OCV-SOC curve based on a sigmoid function, in which only some parameters of the chosen expression vary with cycle aging. In Ref. [23], the authors tested lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) batteries, assessing accelerated calendar aging at 60 °C and modeling the OCV curve with a polynomial function, where only one parameter changes as a result of calendar aging. However, in both studies, the dependency of these parameters on aging was not fully explored. The authors in Ref. [10] analyzed the effects of cycle aging on a lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) battery cell by cycling it at a constant temperature of 30 °C and charging/discharging at a rate of 2.5C within a SOC range of 20–80 %. They developed an OCV-q model consisting of two exponential terms and a constant term. The parameters of the expressions were fitted based on cycle aging, resulting in a complete OCV model that adapts to cell degradation. This model was later adapted to LFP cells in Ref. [24], by adding a linear term to account for the characteristic plateau in the OCV-q curve of LFP batteries. In Ref. [25], the authors aged NCA batteries by storing them at different SOC and temperature conditions, proposing a mechanistic calendar aging model based on the reconstruction of the OCV-SOC curve from degradation modes associated with the electrodes and electrolyte.

Regarding temperature dependency (in a reversible sense), some studies indicate that it has a stronger influence on the OCV curve than aging. To model this dependency, a lookup table was used in Ref. [9] for an LFP battery tested between 0 °C and 50 °C. In Ref. [26], the capacity of lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) batteries was corrected for temperature to adjust the SOC, ensuring that the OCV-SOC curve remained unchanged despite temperature variations between 5 °C and 45 °C. To tackle OCV curve changes due to temperature, a Kalman filter was employed in Ref. [27] for NMC batteries within a temperature range of 5 °C–45 °C, and in Ref. [28] for LFP batteries between 0 °C and 40 °C. In Ref. [29], the authors used Gaussian process regression for LFP batteries operating in the same temperature range of 0 °C–40 °C. In Ref. [30], the OCV-SOC curve was modeled using an 8th-degree polynomial, with each coefficient dependent on temperature, for NMC batteries tested between 5 °C and 40 °C. The authors in Ref. [31] analyzed the variation of the OCV-q curve in an LCO battery at different temperatures between 10 °C and 50 °C, fitting the parameters of the OCV curve expression used in Ref. [10] accordingly. This approach led to the development of a comprehensive model of OCV-q variation as a function of temperature.

Even though temperature appears to be the most influential factor affecting the OCV curve, degradation cannot be neglected. Therefore, it is essential to understand how the combination of these two factors impacts the OCV curve. Several studies in the literature analyze battery cells at different aging levels and temperatures. In Ref. [32], it is stated that by correcting the actual capacity and SOC based on calendar aging and temperature, the OCV-SOC curve remains unchanged under varying conditions. In that study, several LiB chemistries were tested between −25 °C and 45 °C, comparing the OCV curves between the fresh cells with those of the same after one year of calendar aging. In Ref. [33], both temperature and aging effects were investigated, and two separate correction factors were derived. Specifically, the study was conducted on NMC, LFP, and lithium manganese oxide batteries over a temperature range of −20 °C to 45 °C and at various aging states. These aging states included calendar aging at different storage temperatures and cycle aging at fixed temperatures, but instead of applying all aging conditions to the same battery chemistry for a comprehensive analysis, the study applied different aging protocols to different battery types. In Ref. [34], the influence of temperature, cycle aging, and even current rate on the OCV curve was analyzed, and correction factors were introduced to account for these three variables in NMC batteries. The study covered a temperature range of −20 °C to 55 °C and various levels of cycle aging, the latter conducted at room temperature with charging/discharging at a 1C rate. In Ref. [35], a generalized logarithmic OCV curve model was proposed for various battery chemistries, with model parameters fitted at different temperatures (10 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C) and cycle aging levels to capture the observed variations. However, the cycling protocol used in this study was not specified.

Although all these studies consider both temperature and aging, their effects were analyzed separately, without developing a comprehensive model that integrates both factors. Moreover, in most of them, the aging aspect is not consistently or thoroughly investigated—either because it is treated superficially, not well specified, or involves different aging protocols applied to different battery chemistries.

In light of the above, it is important to distinguish between the temperature variations during short-term characterization—used to account for reversible effects—and the temperature conditions applied in the cycle aging protocol, which are intended to capture the irreversible effects of temperature on long-term battery degradation. In the most general case, developing a comprehensive model that accounts for both the reversible and irreversible effects of temperature requires OCV curve characterization at multiple temperatures (to capture reversible effects), as well as subjecting the battery to various aging protocols. These protocols may vary not only in temperature but also in factors such as current rate, SOC windows, and voltage limits. Naturally, this leads to extensive and time-consuming testing. Accordingly, this work takes a first step by fixing the cycle aging protocol to isolate the effects under controlled conditions.

However, within certain limits of temperature, current, SOC windows, and voltage limits, it is reasonable to hypothesize that battery degradation—although progressing at different rates—follows a similar aging path, allowing the characterization to be generalized within these boundaries [36]. To achieve this, an experimental campaign was conducted on LCO cells, in which the OCV curve was measured under different temperatures and cycle aging conditions. The cycle aging was performed using a fixed protocol, with constant battery temperature, SOC window, voltage limits, and cycling rate, to ensure consistency across all aging states. The OCV-q expression from Ref. [10] was used to fit the various curves, and the variation of its parameters with cycle aging and temperature was analyzed. Finally, a comprehensive OCV curve model was developed, incorporating absolute state of discharge, q, battery temperature, T, and cycling level, Q. While the proposed methodology was applied to a specific battery chemistry, LCO, future studies could explore the development of comprehensive OCV curve models for other chemistries to assess their potential for broader implementation, or apply the same approach using different cycle aging protocols beyond the limits discussed above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OCV characterization and cycle aging protocol definition
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the introduction, the cycle aging protocol used in this work is fixed, with controlled conditions for current, voltage limits, SOC windows, and battery temperature. In the literature, numerous studies have investigated these factors, yielding a variety of results that often depend on the specific LiB chemistry examined. However, some general findings are consistent—for instance, within certain temperature limits, batteries tend to follow a similar aging path. Conversely, outside these limits, the dominant aging mechanisms can change, leading to different degradation behaviors and a varying impact on the aging trajectory [37].

Specifically, for the battery temperature-induced aging, it is well-known that typically the effect of temperature on battery degradation exhibits a characteristic V-shape when aging rate is plotted against temperature, consistent with the Arrhenius model, and highlights a specific temperature at which degradation is minimized [38]. This inflection point is understood to mark the transition between two dominant degradation mechanisms—solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer growth and lithium plating. In fact, for temperatures typically higher than 20–25 °C, the predominant degradation mechanism is SEI growth, while at lower temperatures, lithium plating becomes more significant. Specifically, lithium plating tends to occur during charging and increases dramatically at very low temperatures, particularly below 0 °C [39,40].

In [41], the researchers examined the behavior of LCO batteries cycled at 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C using constant current rates of 0.6C and 0.9C introducing a degradation model grounded in the Arrhenius equation. The model showed high accuracy within the 25 °C–35 °C range but diverged at 45 °C, implying the activation of a different degradation mechanism, a finding consistent with observations reported in Ref. [42]. A comparable trend was observed in Ref. [43], where NMC batteries were subjected to cycling at multiple temperatures. In a separate study [44], LFP cells were aged under various thermal conditions (0 °C, 15 °C, 45 °C, and 60 °C), and a capacity fade model based on the Arrhenius law was proposed for temperatures between 15 °C and 60 °C. The data collected at 0 °C were excluded due to their distinct behavior. The results also revealed that at 45 °C, and especially at 60 °C, the empirical model predictions began to diverge from the experimental results, suggesting the emergence of additional degradation mechanisms at elevated temperatures. In any case, batteries are typically thermally managed to operate within an optimal temperature range of approximately 15 °C–20 °C to 35 °C–45 °C [45–47]. Therefore, considering the factors discussed above, a fixed cycle aging protocol at a constant temperature of 30 °C was adopted in this study.

Regarding the influence of current rate, SOC windows, and voltage limits, the study in Ref. [48] demonstrated that for NMC batteries cycled at moderate current rates between 0.5C and 1C, the degradation behavior was similar and independent of the specific current rate. In another study [49], the authors demonstrated that for LCO batteries, capacity degradation was consistent across discharge current rates from C/7 to 2C within the temperature range of 10 °C–45 °C when evaluated as a function of the number of cycles. Specifically, for the same LCO battery used in this work, our earlier studies [50,51] demonstrated that capacity fade was largely independent of current rate (1C–5C) and cycle profile when evaluated as a function of ampere-hour throughput (referred to as total moved charge) provided cycling was conducted at a fixed temperature of 30 °C, within an SOC window of 20–80 %, and voltage limits of 3.45 V–4.05 V. Similar findings were reported in Ref. [52] for NMC batteries.

Besides, especially for electric vehicles, it is recommended to operate the battery within a SOC range of 20 %–80 %, as reported in Ref. [53]. Therefore, considering these factors, a charging/discharging rate of 2C was selected for the cycle aging tests to accelerate the experimental procedure, while ensuring that the influence of current on aging remained negligible. Specifically, the SOC was constrained between 20 % and 80 %, with voltage limits set between 3.5 V and 4 V.

Based on this, we are confident that the proposed OCV model—developed using a fixed cycle aging protocol at 30 °C, with SOC between 20 % and 80 %, voltage between 3.5 V and 4 V, and a current rate of 2C—can be generalized to other aging conditions, provided they remain within the specified limits of current and temperature.

An often overlooked aspect in the literature is that, particularly at high or low battery temperatures, temperature-induced aging during characterization itself may not be negligible. Specifically, when performing OCV characterization at elevated or very low temperatures, the reversible effects of temperature may not be fully separable from the onset of irreversible aging, even over the relatively short duration of the test. Specifically, in Refs. [54,55], a temperature of 60 °C caused significant battery degradation within just a few cycles. On the other hand, as stated in Ref. [18], the OCV typically increases slightly at low temperatures for a given SOC due to changes in the electrochemical potential of the electrodes. At 0 °C and below—particularly during or after charging—lithium plating can occur, even during simple characterization procedures. This phenomenon can lead to permanent capacity loss and distort the true OCV-q relationship. If lithium plating or other side reactions take place during low-temperature testing, the OCV-q curve may shift irreversibly, potentially leading to inaccurate model predictions if not properly accounted for [56,57].

To address this concern, and as previously mentioned, considering that battery thermal management systems typically maintain operation within a temperature range of approximately 15 °C–45 °C, this study conducted OCV curve characterization over a broad and carefully selected temperature range—from 10 °C to 45 °C—that may balance representativeness with minimal interference from temperature-induced degradation.

While calendar aging is quantified over time, cycle aging can be quantified as a function of the cycling level. Following the approach adopted in various studies, the total moved charge, Q (also referred to as ampere-hour throughput), expressed in Ah, can be used:
(4)

Thus, the model (1) can be extended to depend on both the cycling level, Q, and the battery temperature, T.

From an experimental perspective, obtaining the OCV-q curves requires measuring the output battery voltage when the battery has reached equilibrium; otherwise, overpotentials may cause deviations of the output voltage from the true OCV. These overpotentials arise during operation due to the ohmic resistance of the electrodes and electrolyte, charge transfer processes during the intercalation and de-intercalation of lithium ions at the electrodes, and diffusion phenomena within the electrodes and electrolyte. Consequently, it is necessary to eliminate these voltage drops to accurately measure the OCV. To achieve this, two main test procedures are commonly employed in the literature: the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) and pseudo-OCV tests [58,59]. GITT involves applying a sequence of current pulses followed by relaxation periods, during which the battery reaches equilibrium, allowing the OCV to be determined. Pseudo-OCV tests, on the other hand, involve discharging the battery at a very low current to minimize internal overpotentials and approximate the OCV. Although no standard current rate is defined, current rates of ≤0.1C are typically used in the literature [60]. Unfortunately, both methods can be time-consuming, depending on the rest periods required in GITT or the low current rates used in pseudo-OCV tests. In general, while GITT can provide higher accuracy, it is significantly more time-consuming than pseudo-OCV testing [61]. Therefore, we opted to obtain the OCV curves using the pseudo-OCV test performed at 0.1C, as reported in Ref. [62]. This choice represents a good trade-off between time and accuracy, and unlike the GITT, it allows for the acquisition of a continuous OCV curve.

The present analysis was conducted using a pouch-type LiB developed by General Electronics Co., Ltd. Specifically, a 10 Ah LCO pouch cell (model: 8773160K) was used. The main characteristics of the cell are summarized in Table 1 .

The battery was cycled and characterized using the experimental setup shown in Fig. 1 a. The setup includes a potentiostat (SP-150) connected to a booster (VMP3B-100), both manufactured by BioLogic Science Instruments. These tools were controlled using EC-Lab software installed on a PC, which was connected to the devices via Ethernet.

To characterize the battery at different temperatures, three Peltier cells were used, as shown in Fig. 1a. Specifically, one side of each Peltier cell was placed in contact with a heatsink, while the other side was in contact with the bottom side of the LiB cell. The top side of the LiB was left exposed to open air. Furthermore, the three Peltier cells were electrically connected in series and linked to a power converter (BOOSTXL-DRV8323RX) configured as a full-bridge chopper. The battery temperature was controlled by adjusting the current flowing through the Peltier cells as reported in Fig. 1b. This current was regulated by a proportional-integral (PI) regulator implemented in a microcontroller (F28069M). The microcontroller measured the battery temperature using a temperature probe placed on the top side of the battery under test and compared it to a reference temperature value, T
ref . Finally, the duty cycle output from the PI regulator was used to generate the voltage for the Peltier cells via pulse-width modulation. It is worth noting that, although the battery temperature was measured on the top side of the LiB while the Peltier cells were placed on the bottom side, the battery's thin structure allows the temperature to be considered uniform across its volume.

The testing process was divided into two distinct phases: one to measure the discharge OCV-q curves (OCV measurement phase) at different temperatures and the other to age the battery (cycle aging phase). The OCV measurements were performed using the pseudo-OCV test initially at the start of the battery life and then repeated after each cycle aging phase.

The discharge OCV curves were obtained for different fixed temperatures between 10 °C and 45 °C with a temperature step of 5 °C, following a predefined procedure. The battery cell was first fully charged using a constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) protocol, applying a 10 A (1C) current until the voltage reached 4.2 V. The charging process continued in CV mode until the current dropped to 200 mA (0.02C). After achieving a full charge corresponding to 100 % of SOC, the battery was discharged at a low current of 1 A (0.1C) until the voltage decreased to the cutoff value of 2.75 V.

The cycle aging phase was conducted separately at a controlled temperature of 30 °C. This phase consisted of repeated charging and discharging cycles with a higher current of 20 A (2C) to accelerate aging up to move about 5 kAh. As mentioned in previous section and highlighted in Ref. [50], the current rate does not significantly affect the aging process as long as the battery temperature remains stable and the battery operates within the linear range of its OCV-q curve, restricting the voltage to a range of 3.5 V–4.0 V. In this way, cycle aging can be considered dependent solely on the cycling level (i.e., the total moved charge, Q) and independent of the current rate, making the model more general. This cycle aging phase was repeated four times, moving a total of about 20 kAh.

Based on the experimental procedure outlined in the previous section, 40 OCV-q curves were obtained for different battery temperatures and cycling levels. It is important to emphasize that during the measurement of discharge OCV curves, the discharge current was kept very low, minimizing self-heating. As a result, the temperature control system effectively maintained a constant reference temperature, leading to only minor temperature variations during the battery discharge. Nonetheless, the average temperature recorded throughout the discharge process was considered in the analysis. The actual average temperatures along with the related cycling levels are reported in Table 2 .

Since all the pseudo-OCV tests began with the battery fully charged through the CC-CV protocol up to the maximum cutoff voltage, it can be assumed that all the OCV-q curves at q = 0 correspond to 4.2 V. Moreover, taking into account the dependency on the cycling level, Q, and battery temperature, T, model (1) can be rewritten as follows:

The next step involved fitting the experimental data to the proposed model (5) by applying the nonlinear least squares algorithm using the fit function available in MATLAB. Specifically, for each cycling level, Q, and battery temperature, T, the related OCV was fitted as a function of the absolute state of discharge, q. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the comparison between the experimental data and the modeled results indicates a good level of agreement. To assess the overall fit quality, the coefficient of determination (R 2) was calculated:

The purpose of this analysis is to develop a comprehensive V
o (q,T,Q) model capable of predicting the battery OCV for a given absolute state of discharge, q, under varying temperatures, T, and cycling levels, Q. To achieve this, the parameters of (5) need to be fitted as functions of Q and T using appropriate analytical expressions.

Fig. 3 a presents the parameters of (5) as a function of battery temperature, T, across different cycling levels, Q, while Fig. 3b shows the same parameters as a function of cycling level, Q, across different battery temperatures, T. From these figures, it can be observed that parameter p 1 exhibits a well-defined trend, which is almost linear as a function of temperature for each cycling level and quadratic with cycling level, whereas parameter p 2 does not follow a clear pattern in either battery temperature or cycling level. Conversely, parameter λ 1 remains nearly constant across cycling levels but shows a distinct quadratic variation with battery temperature, while λ 2 remains almost constant for each battery temperature and increases with cycling level in a noticeably quadratic manner.

In light of the above, the mean values of λ 1 for each battery temperature, T, and λ 2 for each cycling level, Q, were calculated. Using these averaged values, the fitting process was repeated employing the linear least squares algorithm, as fixing λ 1 and λ 2 for each fitting simplifies the equation, making it linear in its parameters. The simplified model still fits the experimental data very well, as reported in Fig. 4 a and confirmed by the R 2 in Fig. 4b, which remained above 0.991 for all tests.

Fig. 5 a–b illustrate the trend of the parameters after the new fitting. It can be observed that parameter p 2 now exhibits a clearer and more defined trend with respect to both battery temperature and cycling level, while parameter p 1 shows a slightly altered but still well-defined trend, becoming quadratic as a function of battery temperature. Finally, Fig. 5c shows the trends of the parameters: λ 1, varying with battery temperature but fixed for each cycling level, and λ 2, varying with cycling level but fixed for each temperature.

To characterize the proposed comprehensive V
o (q,T,Q) model and assess its generalization capability, we applied the hold-out method by randomly selecting 25 of the 40 OCV-q curves as the characterization subset for model fitting, while the remaining 15 curves were reserved as the validation subset. Therefore, the mean values of λ 1 for each battery temperature, T, and λ 2 for each cycling level, Q, were recalculated for the characterization subset. Using these averaged values, the fitting procedure described in the previous subsection was repeated, yielding similar parameter trends as a function of temperature and cycling level.

As the final step, the parameters p 1 and p 2 were fitted using the following analytical expressions involving two variables, T and Q:

The values of p 1, p 2, λ 1, and λ 2 are shown in Fig. 6 together with their fitting function, while Table 3 lists the coefficients of (7) and (8).

Therefore, by substituting (7) and (8) in (5), the comprehensive V

To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed comprehensive V
o (q,T,Q) model, experimental data from both the characterization and validation subsets were compared with the corresponding values predicted by the model across different battery temperatures, T, and cycling levels, Q. The maximum and mean absolute and relative OCV errors were assessed to quantify the accuracy of the proposed model. Specifically, these OCV errors were determined as follows:

Specifically, in the following figures, the validation points are circled in blue. Fig. 8 a shows both the maximum and mean relative percentage OCV errors calculated along the entire OCV-q curve for different battery temperatures and cycling levels. The results confirm the high accuracy of the proposed model, with the mean relative error remaining below 0.8 % and the maximum error not exceeding 13.5 %.

Nevertheless, the relative OCV percentage error may vary in significance depending on the SOC, as the OCV curve changes with q. Therefore, Fig. 8b presents the maximum and mean absolute error values. From this figure, it can be observed that the maximum OCV errors remain below 350 mV across all tests, while the mean OCV errors stay below 30 mV. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the proposed model effectively handles both interpolation within the characterization range and extrapolation beyond it.

The proposed V
o (q,T,Q) model offers a method to estimate the actual battery capacity, C
a , and update the SOC using equation (3). By rearranging equation (5) to solve for q and setting the battery output voltage to the minimum cutoff value of 2.75 V, the corresponding q value provides an estimate of C
a , for a given battery temperature, T, and cycling level, Q. To assess the accuracy of this prediction, the relative error of the battery capacity estimation was computed using the following formula:

where q

Fig. 8c presents the relative error values for battery capacity, indicating that the capacity estimation achieves high accuracy with errors remaining below 2.5 %.

From a physical perspective, according to Ref. [63], the high voltage region of the OCV-q curve is primarily governed by the open-circuit potential (OCP) of the cathode, while the low voltage region is dominated by the anode OCP. The intermediate portion of the curve (medium voltage region) reflects an overlap of the two contributions. Within the proposed analytical model, the first exponential term characterizes the high-to-medium voltage region of the OCV-q curve and can therefore be associated with the cathode OCP, whereas the second exponential term describes the low-to-medium voltage region of the curve and can be attributed to the anode OCP.

As mentioned in the introduction, both the temperature (in reversible sense) and degradation mechanisms change the OCV-q curve. The proposed model demonstrated the capability to capture these variations through four parameters, which are functions of the battery temperature, T, and cycle aging, quantified by the moved charge, Q. From an analytical standpoint, the parameters p 1 and p 2 act as translation factors, shifting the exponential functions along the q-axis and the V
o -axis, while ensuring that the intersection of V
o (q) with the V
o -axis remains constant at 4.2 V. Conversely, the parameters λ 1 and λ 2 regulate the exponential decay rates, thereby determining the steepness of the corresponding functions.

Specifically, an increase (decrease) in p 1 primarily shifts the high-to-medium voltage region of the OCV-q curve downward (upward), while an increase (decrease) in λ 1 stretches (shortens) this region along the q-axis, thereby raising (lowering) its overall profile. Similarly, an increase (decrease) in p 2 shifts the knee in the low voltage region of the OCV−q curve to the left (right), whereas an increase (decrease) in λ 2 compresses (stretches) the curve along the q-axis, thereby shifting the knee to the right (left).

Specifically, Fig. 4 shows that increasing temperature raises the voltage in the high-to-medium voltage region of the OCV-q curve; that is, for the same absolute state of discharge, q, the OCV is higher. This behavior is captured in the model by the dominant effect of the increase in λ 1 (Fig. 6) relative to the increase in p 1 (Fig. 5a) as the temperature rises. In contrast, the low-to-medium voltage region of the OCV curve aligns with the trend reported in Refs. [64,65] for battery capacity as a function of temperature, where capacity increases with temperature up to a threshold (between 35 and 40 °C in this case), after which it decreases as the temperature continues to rise. The initial increase can be explained by the Arrhenius relationship, which predicts that reaction rates accelerate with increasing temperature [19]. This enhances lithium-ion intercalation and deintercalation, thereby contributing to higher apparent capacity. Elevated temperatures also improve ionic concentration and mobility in the electrolyte, further facilitating these processes. Beyond the threshold, however, the measured capacity decreases, likely because excessive thermal energy induces overly rapid ion motion, which impedes efficient intercalation and deintercalation [65]. This overall effect is reflected in the model by the parameter p 2 (Fig. 5a), which follows the same trend as battery capacity with respect to temperature, while λ 2 remains constant.

On the other hand, Fig. 4 shows that cycle aging compresses the OCV-q curve along both the q-axis and the V
o
−axis. Specifically, for a given absolute state of discharge, q, the voltage decreases as cycling level, Q, increases. This effect is represented in the model primarily through the parameter p 1, which increases with increasing Q, while λ 1 remains constant. In the low voltage region of the OCV-q curve, the knee shifts toward lower q, reflecting the reduction in battery capacity with increasing cycling level, Q. This behavior is captured in the model mainly by the parameter λ 2, which increases with Q.

It is well established that various battery aging mechanisms give rise to specific aging modes, including the loss of lithium inventory (LLI) and the loss of active material (LAM) [66]. LLI is due to the reduction of active lithium available for intercalation and deintercalation reactions in a battery and during the initial phase of a battery life is mainly caused by SEI formation and growth developing of a stable SEI layer, which then decreases gradually as the battery continues to cycle. Moreover, during further cycle, SEI can cracks and reforms yielding to additional LLI. Another key contributor to LLI is lithium plating, which occurs when metallic lithium deposits on the anode surface during charging, particularly under low-temperature conditions. Some of the deposited lithium can react further with the electrolyte, leading to the formation of additional SEI. This, in turn, can promote further lithium plating, leading to a self-reinforcing mechanism as reported in Ref. [67]. In contrast, LAM arises from the loss of electrical or ionic conductivity of active material particles within the electrodes, the dissolution of transition metals into the electrolyte, changes in electrode composition, and structural alterations in the active material, all of which ultimately diminish its ability to undergo lithiation.

It is noteworthy that, according to Ref. [68], LLI manifests as a shift in the anode OCP, whereas LAM appears as a stretching of both the anode and cathode OCP. The parameter trends observed in the V
o (q,T,Q) model proposed in this work may therefore serve as indicators of the occurrence of LLI and LAM within the battery. Specifically, parameter λ 2 may indicate the threshold beyond which the self-reinforcing mechanism between SEI growth and lithium plating occurs—around 15 kAh in this case—where the cubic polynomial fitting function shows an increased rate of change.

In this study, the OCV-q model previously introduced in Refs. [10,31], which separately accounted for the influence of cycling levels and battery temperature, was extended to integrate both effects into a comprehensive model. A series of experiments were conducted through pseudo-OCV tests, measuring OCV curves at various temperatures using Peltier cells for thermal control and under different cycling levels.

The model comprises five parameters, but to simplify the analysis, one parameter was fixed based on the assumption that all tests began with a fully charged battery, ensuring that the battery maximum voltage was 4.2 V.

The fitting process yielded high accuracy, with the remaining four parameters dependent on both battery temperature and cycling levels. From these results, it was observed that parameter p
2 did not exhibit a well-defined trend, whereas parameter λ 1 remained relatively constant across different cycling levels, while λ 2 showed consistency across various temperatures.

To further simplify the model, parameter λ 1 was treated as dependent solely on battery temperature, while λ 2 was considered dependent only on cycling levels. With these constraints, the fitting process was repeated, resulting in updated values for p
1 and p
2 across different battery temperatures and cycling levels. The revised model maintained high accuracy in modeling the experimental OCV-q curves, with parameter p
2 now showing a more distinct and well-defined trend. Afterward, the experimental data were divided into two subsets—characterization and validation—according to the hold-out method. The proposed model was first fitted using the characterization subset and then validated against both the characterization and validation data. Consequently, parameters p
1 and p
2 were fitted as functions of battery temperature and cycling level using two-variable polynomial functions, while λ 1 and λ 2 were modeled using single-variable polynomial functions.

The model accuracy was verified by comparing modeled and experimental data, with the relative OCV errors evaluated to determine the modeling quality. Notably, the mean relative OCV error was below 0.8 % across all tests. Finally, battery capacity was estimated, and the relative error in this estimation was calculated, yielding values under 2.5 % for all battery temperatures and cycling levels. Moreover, the model demonstrated reliable accuracy in both interpolation and extrapolation across various temperatures and cycling levels, validating its suitability as a robust predictive tool.

These results demonstrate that the proposed V
o (q,T,Q) model effectively can estimate capacity degradation across a range of battery temperatures. Furthermore, it can be applied to estimate and update the battery SOC under various battery temperature and cycle aging conditions based on the estimation of the actual battery capacity.

Although the proposed methodology was applied to an LCO battery, different battery chemistries may result in different OCV curve shapes. However, the authors believe that cobalt-based lithium-ion batteries, such as NMC and NCA batteries, are likely to exhibit similar behavior, allowing the use of the same OCV-q analytical function. For other chemistries, such as LFP, which display a characteristic plateau in the OCV curve, the model would require adjustments. Nonetheless, the proposed methodology would still remain applicable.
